basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Shams — “The Sun” — is perhaps the sūra where the Quran strings the greatest number of oaths in a row. Eleven of them, in seven verses, raising the universe itself as a witness: sun and its brightness, moon that follows, day that makes shine forth, night that veils, heaven and He who built it, earth and He who spread it, soul and He who fashioned it in harmony. Eleven oaths to say one thing: blessed is the one who purifies his soul, wretched the one who debases it. The whole cosmos is summoned to bear witness to the fate of a single heart. Qushayrī, at the end of the sūra, gives what he calls its fruit (fāʾida) — a summary of gathered beauty: “Blessed is the one who has purified his soul of sins and defects, then of greed for compensations and calculations, then has kept his soul from objecting to the divine apportionings and from yielding to the forbidden. And wretched the one who has betrayed his soul, neglected its care, stained it with transgressions, and who — not content with lacking the inward savour (al-maʿānī, المَعَانِي) — has added to its poverty obscure pretensions: then his ship has sunk in the sea of wretchedness.” At the heart of the sūra stands verse 8 — one of the deepest in the Quran on the nature of the soul: He inspired in it its transgression and its godfearing. Both ways lie within the soul; they lie within it because God Himself has inspired them there. Now, to each, the choice of which to listen to.

versets.trad: By the sun and its brightness!
versets.commentaire: al-shams (الشَّمْس): the sun. ḍuḥā (ضُحَى) — already met in sūra 93 — names here more precisely the very beginning of the moment of sunrise, says Qushayrī: “the ḍuḥā of the sun is the first instant of its rising.” The sūra begins by swearing not by the sun alone, but by the sun and its brightness. The nuance matters: the oath bears on the star as it shines, on the couple of source and its gift. All that follows in the sūra will be of this kind: one does not swear by things, but by things in the very motion by which they manifest.

mots.def: ḍuḥā: the very beginning of sunrise, morning brightness; with feminine suffix pronoun.

versets.trad: By the moon when it follows it!
versets.commentaire: talāhā (تَلَاهَا): it followed it. Qushayrī: “It follows the sun and stays low before it during the first half of the month” — that is, the moon appears behind the sun and grows as the sun fades. Tilāwa, of the same root, names also the following recitation of the Quran. In both cases, it is the gesture of coming after while keeping the measure. The moon does not go before the sun; it does not vie with it; it receives its light and gives it back. A silent image of the faithful servant.

mots.def: talā: to follow; also “to recite (in faithful sequence of the text).” With feminine suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: By the day when it makes it shine forth!
versets.commentaire: jallāhā (جَلَّاهَا): it brought it forth in clarity, unveiled it. Same root as tajallā in 92:2: manifestation as unveiling. Qushayrī, soberly: “when it manifested the sun and uncovered it.” The day, then, is not a light next to the sun; the day is what unveils the sun, what makes it visible to the creature. The nuance is exquisite: what illumines is not only the source — it is also the medium through which that source reaches us.

mots.def: jallā: intensive form of jalā; to manifest, to unveil, to bring forth in clarity.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: By the night when it covers it!
versets.commentaire: yaghshāhā (يَغْشَاهَا): it veils it, covers it. The verb is the same as in 92:1, but here the sense is wholly different: it is the night that covers the sun — that veils its light by absorbing it into shadow. In the grammar of the oaths, sun and night are set side by side: not to oppose one another, but to say that all light knows its veil. The Sufis will hear in this the very rhythm of the spiritual life: expansion (basṭ) gives way to contraction (qabḍ), brightness to obscurity — and the servant, in both, learns to recognise the same hand.

mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: By the heaven and He who built it!
versets.commentaire: The pronoun mā (مَا) bears, as in sūra 92, the deliberate ambiguity: it can be rendered as “that which” (the heaven and its building) or as “He who” (the heaven and He who built it). Qushayrī leans on the second reading: “He who built it” is God Himself. The oath no longer rests on created things alone — it embraces their Creator. A splendid pivot: from the testimony of things one passes to God's testimony of Himself through His works.

mots.def: neuter relative pronoun, here functioning as 'He who'.
banā: to build, to construct; with feminine suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: By the earth and He who spread it out!
versets.commentaire: ṭaḥā (طَحَا): to spread out, to unfold, to apportion — the earth seen as a cloth unrolled, or apportioned. Qushayrī: “He who spread it (unfolded it) or apportioned it or created it.” The heaven rises, the earth spreads out. Both bear together the life of man: a roofless sky, a bordlerless earth. And between them, the creature now to be named.

mots.def: neuter relative pronoun, here functioning as 'He who'.
ṭaḥā: to spread out, to unfold, to apportion; with feminine suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: By the soul and He who fashioned it in harmony!
versets.commentaire: The summit of the oaths. sawwāhā (سَوَّاهَا): He made it equal, He harmonised it, He set in balance all its parts. Qushayrī, briefly: “He who ordered its elements and its members.” The nafs (نَفْس) — the soul — is presented thus not as a given thing, but as a divine work of adjustment. Its whole inner architecture is held in a harmony that is not its own. It is upon this ground of adjusted harmony that the next verse will speak of the two ways within it: the possible imbalance and the balance to maintain.

mots.def: nafs: soul, breath, inner self; indefinite genitive.
neuter relative pronoun, here functioning as 'He who'.
sawwā: intensive form of sawiya; to make equal, to harmonise, to adjust; with feminine suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: He inspired in it its transgression and its godfearing;
versets.commentaire: Pivotal verse — one of the deepest in the Quran on the nature of the soul. alhamahā (أَلْهَمَهَا): He inspired it, He cast down within it. Fujūr (فُجُور): transgression, overflowing (the river that breaks its banks). Taqwā (تَقْوى): godfearing, reverent vigilance. Both are named as “inspired” by God — which raises a vertiginous mystery. Qushayrī gives three readings that coexist: (1) He inspired the two by abandoning the soul to one or keeping it for the other; (2) fujūr is the soul's movement in seeking sustenance; taqwā, its stillness under the divine decree; (3) simply: “the way of good and that of evil.” The soul knows, from within itself, the two ways — not by deduction, but by direct inspiration. The choice lies within because the two options lie within. The sūra does not ask man to discover what he ought to do; it presumes he knows. It only asks him to do it.

mots.def: alhama: to inspire (to breathe into the heart without intermediary); same root as ilhām, inward inspiration.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: fujūr: transgression, overflowing (same root as fajr, the dawn that breaks the night, and infijār, bursting forth); with suffix pronoun.
taqwā: godfearing, reverent vigilance; with suffix pronoun.

versets.trad: blessed is the one who purifies it,
versets.commentaire: Here at last is the answer to the eleven oaths — the sentence for which the whole cosmos has been summoned. aflaḥa (أَفْلَحَ): he has prospered, he is blessed; same root as falāḥ, spiritual prospering. zakkāhā (زَكَّاهَا): he purified it, he made it grow. Qushayrī acknowledges two grammatical readings: “Blessed is he who purified his soul”; or “Blessed is he whom God, glorified and exalted, purified.” The beautiful Arabic ambiguity allows both: purification is at once a work man performs and a gift made to him. As nearly always in spirituality: what depends on man and what depends on God meet in one same movement.

mots.def: particle of affirmation for the perfect.
aflaḥa: to prosper, to succeed spiritually; same root as falāḥ.
zakkā: intensive form of zakā; to purify, to make grow. Same root as zakāt.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: and wretched the one who debases it!
versets.commentaire: khāba (خَابَ): he failed, was disappointed in his hopes. dassāhā (دَسَّاهَا) — a rare and laden verb. Its original form is dassasahā (دَسَّسَهَا), “he buried it”; one letter has been substituted for euphonic relief. Dasasa, to hide one thing within another, to bury secretly. Qushayrī offers four readings: (1) “God buried it”; (2) “he hid it among the righteous though he was not of them”; (3) “he buried his soul through disobedience to God”; (4) “he made it base, vile, contemptible.” All these readings converge: to debase the soul is to hide it, to bury it under layers heaped on top of it. Sin does not destroy the soul — it buries it. The way of purification is, conversely, a long movement of unburying.

mots.def: khāba: to be disappointed in one's hope, to fail, to perish.
dassā (originally dassasa): to bury, to hide within, to bury in secret; hence: to debase.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: Thamūd, in their revolt, cried lies,
versets.commentaire: The sūra, having stated the general law, now illustrates its underside by a historical example. Thamūd, an ancient Arabian people to whom the prophet Ṣāliḥ was sent (cf. 7:73-79; 11:61-68). Bi-ṭaghwāhā (بِطَغْوَاهَا): “by their own overflowing” — the verb ṭaghā, already met in 96:6, names the water that bursts its banks, the pride that no longer holds. Qushayrī here reports a striking tradition: “It is said that Ṣāliḥ had died, and that his people disbelieved; God brought him back to life, and he called them to faith; they denied him, and asked him for a sign — which was the she-camel.” Whether historical or not, this tradition tells a truth of the soul: there are denials so stubborn that even a prophet returned from the dead cannot bend them.

mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, to hold as a lie; in the feminine (subject: Thamūd, the tribe).
Thamūd: name of the ancient Arabian tribe, people of the prophet Ṣāliḥ.
ṭaghwā: overflowing, revolt, breach of measure; with feminine suffix pronoun.

versets.trad: when the most wretched among them rose up…
versets.commentaire: ashqāhā (أَشْقاهَا): “the most wretched among them” — Qushayrī names him: “the one who hamstrung the she-camel.” Tradition reports it was a man named Qudār ibn Sālif. In filigree the verse states a subtle truth: it is not the majority that acts; it is the most wretched, the one whom shaqāwa had already eaten away, who rises up. And all the others fall in behind him. The evil of a people is often less the work of the mass than the work of one man, followed by the silence of the others.

mots.sens: he rose up, he sprang up
mots.def: inbaʿatha: derived form of baʿatha; to rise, to spring up.
ashqā: superlative of shaqī; the most wretched.

versets.ar: فَقَالَ لَهُمْ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ نَاقَةَ اللَّهِ وَسُقْيَاهَا
versets.tr: fa-qāla lahum rasūlu Llāhi nāqata Llāhi wa-suqyāhā
versets.trad: The Messenger of God had told them: “The she-camel of God!… Let her drink!…”
versets.commentaire: The she-camel (nāqa, نَاقَة) was the sign God sent to Thamūd at their request, by the hand of the prophet Ṣāliḥ. The Quran tells elsewhere (7:73; 26:155) that she was to drink one day, and Thamūd the next. Qushayrī sums up the warning: “Beware God's she-camel, and beware her drinking time — that is, do not stand in her way.” The verse is short, but its scope is immense: what is of God must be respected precisely in its modest demand, and not only in its splendour.

mots.def: suqyā: the act of giving drink, the right to drink; with feminine suffix pronoun.

versets.ar: فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَعَقَرُوهَا فَدَمْدَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُم بِذَنبِهِمْ فَسَوَّاهَا
versets.tr: fa-kadhdhabūhu fa-ʿaqarūhā fa-damdama ʿalayhim rabbuhum bi-dhanbihim fa-sawwāhā
versets.trad: They denied him and hamstrung the she-camel. Their Lord then destroyed them, every last one of them, for their sin,
versets.commentaire: Three successive fa-'s (“then… then… then…”) mark the implacable sequence: they deny, they kill, He annihilates. damdama (دَمْدَمَ): He brought down upon them the chastisement in one stroke. fa-sawwāhā (فَسَوَّاهَا), “He equalised it”: Qushayrī gives two beautifully complementary senses: (1) “the ground was levelled over them” — as though God closed the land upon them as one closes a book; (2) “He made them equal in the chastisement, because they had all consented to the hamstringing of the she-camel.” Subtle grammatical note: the verb sawwā is the same as in verse 7 (where God orders the soul in harmony). The soul disharmonised by sin is, in the end, re-harmonised in chastisement — no longer as grace, but as justice.

mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: ʿaqara: to cut the hamstrings of, to slaughter (a mount); with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: damdama: to bring down a chastisement in one stroke.
dhanb: sin, fault; with suffix pronoun.
sawwā: to equalise, to level; here, either to level the land over them, or to equalise their fate in chastisement.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: and their disappearance gives Him no apprehension.
versets.commentaire: ʿuqbā (عُقْبَى): consequence, what follows an affair. Qushayrī: “God does not fear the consequence of what He has done to them as chastisement.” The verse is sober, but it says something fundamental about divine sovereignty: God has neither to justify Himself nor to fear that His act will turn against Him. What for man is heavy with consequences is not so for Him. And Qushayrī closes his reading of the sūra on his magnificent fruit (fāʾida, فَائِدَة), summed up in a few lines: “Blessed is the one who has purified his soul of sins and defects, then of greed for compensations and calculations, then has kept it from objecting to the divine apportionings and from yielding to the forbidden. And wretched the one who has betrayed his soul, neglected its care, stained it with transgressions, and who — not content with lacking the inward savour (al-maʿānī, المَعَانِي) — has added to it obscure pretensions: then his ship has sunk in the sea of wretchedness.”

mots.def: ʿuqbā: consequence, what follows an affair; with feminine suffix pronoun.
